title: DRY EYES AFTER BOTOX - How to Avoid & treat This Botox Complication
filename: DRY EYES AFTER BOTOX - How to Avoid & treat This Botox Complication.en.vtt
excerpt: The next side effect that you must learn to avoid is dry eye so dry eye sounds like a mild side effect but I've suffered this myself after an eye injury I was working on some wood once and a bit broke off a router and effect and hit me in the eye I then had dry eye that had to tr…

segments.text: the next side effect that you must learn to avoid is dry eye so dry eye sounds like a mild side effect but I've suffered this myself after an eye injury I was working on some wood once and a bit broke off a router and effect and hit me in the eye I then had dry eye that had to treat for about a month putting drops into my eye and it was miserable it really gets in the way of a lot of day-to-day function and if you do this to your patients it's certainly not to be dismissed as a mild side effect but thankfully it's rare and also easy to avoid let's understand what could be causing a dry eye it starts by understanding the position of the lacr mod land if you have a look in this 3D diagram you can see the position of the lacrimal gland just near the suture between the zygomatic bone and the frontal bone I think in many people it's probably more Superior than this when I've looked on cavas but the important point is it is in an area where we do inject there are two ways you can affect this you could just be doing a lateral canthal treatment treating lateral canthal lines with a 4unit dose here that happens to be a bit close to the eye but there's another injection I do

very frequently which is treating underneath the eyebrow where I'm affecting the orbicularis oculi trying to get a little bit of lateral lift so how do we avoid these injections causing the complication the first thing is to think about that orbital Rim as one of the boundaries that you always check and leave if you're treating lateral Canal lines the 1.5 CM margin and the second principle applies to both areas whether you're treating underneath the eyebrow or the lateral canle lines it's all about depth as we talked about before bicular oculi which is the muscle we're trying to treat is superficial it's underneath a thin layer of skin and underneath the muscle is fat and those layers can be used to defend the tear land from bot toxin so we're 1.5 CM away or very superficial shielded by the layers of tissue which defend the eye so defending the lacr gland is ultimately about being out of the tissue plane that could be affected so if you are superior to it but shielded by the muscle a layer of fat and the orbital septum you going to be

much less likely to cause a dry eye if you're injecting lateral canor lines you also have the ability to leave a 1.5 CM margin which also makes it much less likely that you'll get anywhere near that structure the next area to think about which is more about side effects than really a complication it's an unwanted effect is when you overtreat the lower lateral aspect of the eye this happens when treating lateral canthal lines so trying to get rid of crow's feet if you treat a little bit too heavily you can get relaxation of orbicularis oculi that then meets the transition point of the zygomaticus major as it pulls up and then causes a little ledge so the patient will present a new Shadow or ledge on their lid that they don't like when they smile and this means they're not happy even though you've clearly got rid of all their lines and wrinkles now how do we get rid of this problem it's really a matter of dose and if you reduce your dose on the lower aspect of the eyelid I only use two units for nearly all my patients now you get a great result without causing Ledges and without causing some of the next side effect which I'm going to discuss so the next side effect um as

described by Julie Bass Kaplan is looking a bit like a psycho now what's going on with this I had Botox under my eyes here and didn't realize that my time was over for Botox under my eyes right like so every time I smiled like a psychopath like I was smiling like this the whole time right it's all down to Descent of the lower lid if your lower lid descends down you look a little bit too wide-eyed and as Julie describes this makes you look a little bit scary in certain instances and you will see this on many injectors and it comes from overtreating underneath the eye as you get older and you lose collagen and elastin if you're treating an area that is basically held together by the muscle and you relax that muscle you will get Descent of the low lid and it's even though the lines and wrinkles may be gone it just doesn't look that attractive so this is quite a common side effect that's easily avoided essentially by patient selection so make sure you choose patients who have a good SNAP test clearly have plenty of collagen elastin and use low Doses and

you shouldn't have this problem it's also worth consenting your patients into understanding the difference between getting rid of lines and wrinkles and actually looking better because sometimes you do achieve one outcome but you don't get the other finally there's a very rare complication and this actually happens in really old people even if they've never had bot lime toxin which is the skin quality deteriorate so much that you actually see the pink of the lower lid so this is called an neopian eyelid and showing pink underneath the eye is a side effect of that tissue that is unable to hold itself in position but if you add boty toxin to someone who's nearly at that point you can see how that would bring this problem on so the orbicular oculi is actually holding the lower eyelid against the lower part of the globe and as you relax it to try and get rid of a line it then Falls outward and you end up with a very unattractive but very rare complication I've never actually seen a patient get this though I've read about it and it comes from poor patient selection and very high doses of but toxin in the lower lid So to avoid this complication basically I do very little treatment underneath the eye I think

it's only suitable for a very small minority of patients so the reason to do it is not strong in my opinion I think it sometimes helps certain patients with very crinkly skin but they're quite young and you can soften it with a very low dose and but for most patients who have lines under their eyes are actually caused by the zygomatic muscles and the the cheek elevators which squeeze the lower eyelid skin during a smile and so treating orbicular oculi is only going to have a minimal effect anyway and sometimes it even makes it worse so patient selection and what I'm basically saying is I don't think it's a great treatment anyway so try not to do it unless you've got a very clear reason why you think it's going to work on your particular patient
